questionText: "Money is the root of all evil."
insightText: The actual verse is 1 Timothy 6:10 (NIV): "For the love of money is a root of all kinds of evil." The misquote drops "the love of" and changes "a root of all kinds of evil" to "the root of all evil."

questionText: "God helps those who help themselves."
insightText: This was first written by English politician Algernon Sidney in *Discourses Concerning Government* (1698) and later popularized by Benjamin Franklin in *Poor Richard's Almanack* (1736). The Bible emphasizes God helping the helpless (Psalm 72:12-13).

questionText: "Speak up for those who cannot speak for themselves."
insightText: Proverbs 31:8 (NIV): "Speak up for those who cannot speak for themselves, for the rights of all who are destitute." A direct mandate for advocacy and compassion.

questionText: "Hate the sin, love the sinner."
insightText: This comes from St. Augustine's Letter 211 (c. 424 AD): "Cum dilectione hominum et odio vitiorum" — "With love for mankind and hatred of sins." It was later adapted by Mahatma Gandhi in his 1929 autobiography.

questionText: "If your enemy is hungry, feed him."
insightText: Romans 12:20 (NIV): "On the contrary: 'If your enemy is hungry, feed him; if he is thirsty, give him something to drink. In doing this, you will heap burning coals on his head.'" Also found in Proverbs 25:21.

questionText: "Cleanliness is next to godliness."
insightText: This phrase was popularized by John Wesley in his sermon "On Dress" (Sermon 88, 1791). The concept has roots in ancient Hebrew and Babylonian writings, but this exact phrase is not in the Bible.

questionText: "Religion that God accepts as pure is this: to look after orphans and widows in their distress."
insightText: James 1:27 (NIV): "Religion that God our Father accepts as pure and faultless is this: to look after orphans and widows in their distress and to keep oneself from being polluted by the world."

questionText: "This too shall pass."
insightText: This is an ancient Persian proverb, often attributed to Persian Sufi poets such as Attar of Nishapur. It was later popularized in the West by Abraham Lincoln in an 1859 address. It does not appear anywhere in the Bible.

questionText: "God will not give you more than you can handle."
insightText: This is a misquote of 1 Corinthians 10:13 (NIV): "No temptation has overtaken you except what is common to mankind. And God is faithful; he will not let you be tempted beyond what you can bear." The verse is about *temptation*, not suffering. The Bible is full of people who suffered beyond their own capacity (2 Corinthians 1:8).

questionText: "Carry each other's burdens."
insightText: Galatians 6:2 (NIV): "Carry each other's burdens, and in this way you will fulfill the law of Christ."

questionText: "Charity begins at home."
insightText: This phrase comes from Sir Thomas Browne's *Religio Medici* (1642): "Charity begins at home, is the voice of the world." It does not appear in the Bible, though 1 Timothy 5:8 urges providing for one's household.

questionText: "He who shuts his ear to the cry of the poor will also cry himself and not be answered."
insightText: Proverbs 21:13 (NIV): "Whoever shuts their ears to the cry of the poor will also cry out and not be answered." A harsh warning about ignoring those in need.

questionText: "To thine own self be true."
insightText: This is a line spoken by Polonius in William Shakespeare's *Hamlet* (Act 1, Scene 3, c. 1600): "This above all: to thine own self be true."

questionText: "A bruised reed he will not break."
insightText: Isaiah 42:3 (NIV): "A bruised reed he will not break, and a smoldering wick he will not snuff out. In faithfulness he will bring forth justice." Also quoted by Jesus in Matthew 12:20.

questionText: "The lion shall lay down with the lamb."
insightText: This is a common misquote. Isaiah 11:6 (NIV) actually says: "The wolf will live with the lamb, the leopard will lie down with the goat, the calf and the lion and the yearling together; and a little child will lead them." It's the *wolf*, not the lion, with the lamb.

questionText: "Do unto others as you would have them do unto you."
insightText: Luke 6:31 (NIV): "Do to others as you would have them do to you." Known as the Golden Rule. Also found in Matthew 7:12.

questionText: "Spare the rod, spoil the child."
insightText: This exact phrasing is from Samuel Butler's satirical poem *Hudibras* (1664): "Love is a boy by poets styl'd; Then spare the rod and spoil the child." It is loosely based on Proverbs 13:24 (NIV): "Whoever spares the rod hates their children, but the one who loves their children is careful to discipline them."

questionText: "Let him who is without sin cast the first stone."
insightText: John 8:7 (NIV): "When they kept on questioning him, he straightened up and said to them, 'Let any one of you who is without sin be the first to throw a stone at her.'"

questionText: "Pride goes before a fall."
insightText: Proverbs 16:18 (NIV): "Pride goes before destruction, a haughty spirit before a fall." The common saying is a shortened version of this verse.

questionText: "Neither a borrower nor a lender be."
insightText: This is spoken by Polonius in William Shakespeare's *Hamlet* (Act 1, Scene 3, c. 1600): "Neither a borrower nor a lender be, for loan oft loses both itself and friend." The Bible actually discusses lending in Deuteronomy 15:8 and Psalm 37:26.

questionText: "Blessed are the merciful, for they will be shown mercy."
insightText: Matthew 5:7 (NIV): "Blessed are the merciful, for they will be shown mercy." Part of Jesus' Sermon on the Mount (the Beatitudes).

questionText: "God works in mysterious ways."
insightText: This comes from William Cowper's hymn *Light Shining out of Darkness* (1773), commonly known as "God Moves in a Mysterious Way." The opening line: "God moves in a mysterious way, His wonders to perform." It is not a Bible verse.

questionText: "A gentle answer turns away wrath."
insightText: Proverbs 15:1 (NIV): "A gentle answer turns away wrath, but a harsh word stirs up anger."

questionText: "Be kind, for everyone you meet is fighting a hard battle."
insightText: This quote is widely attributed to Ian Maclaren (pen name of Rev. John Watson), a Scottish author and minister, from around 1897. It is often misattributed to Plato. It does not appear in the Bible.

questionText: "As iron sharpens iron, so one person sharpens another."
insightText: Proverbs 27:17 (NIV): "As iron sharpens iron, so one person sharpens another."

questionText: "Weeping may stay for the night, but rejoicing comes in the morning."
insightText: Psalm 30:5 (NIV): "For his anger lasts only a moment, but his favor lasts a lifetime; weeping may stay for the night, but rejoicing comes in the morning."

questionText: "The eye is the lamp of the body."
insightText: Matthew 6:22 (NIV): "The eye is the lamp of the body. If your eyes are healthy, your whole body will be full of light."

questionText: "A fool and his money are soon parted."
insightText: This proverb is attributed to Thomas Tusser's *Five Hundred Points of Good Husbandry* (1573). While the Bible has much to say about foolishness and wealth (Proverbs 21:20), this exact phrase is not in Scripture.

questionText: "The truth will set you free."
insightText: John 8:32 (NIV): "Then you will know the truth, and the truth will set you free." Jesus said this to the Jews who believed in him.

questionText: "Love your neighbor and hate your enemy."
insightText: Jesus quotes this cultural saying in Matthew 5:43-44 (NIV) to correct it: "You have heard that it was said, 'Love your neighbor and hate your enemy.' But I tell you, love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you." The "hate your enemy" part was a popular addition — it is not found in the Old Testament.
